skill.name: slack-gif-creator
skill.description: Knowledge and utilities for creating animated GIFs optimized for Slack. Provides constraints, validation tools, and animation concepts. Use when users request animated GIFs for Slack.
skill.summary: A toolkit for creating animated GIFs optimized for Slack messaging with dimension constraints, validation tools, and easing functions.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 64 static findings are false positives. The skill is a legitimate GIF creation utility using standard Python libraries (PIL, numpy, imageio). No network calls, no external command execution, no credential access, and no data exfiltration. Static scanner was triggered by keywords in license text and code documentation.

high_findings.title: False Positive: C2 Keywords in Animation Constants
high_findings.description: Variable names c1, c2, c3 in easing.py are animation easing constants (1.70158), not C2 (command-and-control) indicators. Standard animation math formulas.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The variables c1, c2, c3 are standard easing function coefficients used in back ease formulas - confirmed by mathematical context

high_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
high_findings.description: Scanner detected imports at line 5 but these are standard image processing libraries (PIL, numpy), not cryptographic algorithms. No cryptography used.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard Python libraries for image processing - PIL/numpy are not cryptographic. License file triggered by standard legal text about cryptography.

high_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance
high_findings.description: Scanner detected 'import math' as system reconnaissance. This is standard Python math library for calculating polygon points in frame_composer.py.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The math module is used for star polygon point calculations - standard image processing, not reconnaissance

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded URLs
medium_findings.description: URLs in LICENSE.txt are standard Apache License URLs (apache.org), not suspicious network indicators.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard Apache License URLs in license file - expected and legitimate

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Dynamic Import Expression
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md shows Python import statements for the skill's own modules (core.frame_composer), not dynamic code loading.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Documentation showing legitimate module imports from the skill's own core package

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution
medium_findings.description: Scanner detected backticks in SKILL.md but these are Markdown code fences (```python), not shell execution. The backtick detection is a scanner false positive.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are Python code examples in Markdown fenced code blocks - standard documentation, not shell commands

content.value_statement: Slack has strict requirements for animated GIFs including size limits and dimension constraints that are difficult to navigate manually. This skill provides a complete toolkit with GIFBuilder, validation tools, and easing functions to create optimized GIFs that work perfectly in Slack channels.
actual_capabilities: Create animated GIFs with precise dimension control (128x128 for emoji, 480x480 for messages)
Build frames programmatically using PIL drawing primitives (circles, polygons, stars, text)
Apply easing functions for smooth natural motion (bounce, elastic, back, ease-in-out)
Optimize GIF file size through color quantization and duplicate frame removal
Validate GIFs against Slack requirements before sharing
limitations: Does not render video files to GIF (requires external video tools)
No pre-built animation templates - must create animation logic from primitives
Does not include emoji fonts (unreliable across platforms)
Cannot upload GIFs directly to Slack - generates file for manual upload

use_cases.title: Create Custom Slack Emoji
use_cases.description: Generate animated emoji GIFs at exactly 128x128 with optimized colors and smooth animations for team communication.
use_cases.target_user: Slack team members who want custom animated reactions

use_cases.title: Build Visual Announcements
use_cases.description: Create animated message GIFs for product launches, announcements, or celebrations that meet Slack size limits.

use_cases.title: Develop Animation Prototypes
use_cases.description: Prototype animated graphics concepts programmatically before sending to design tools for final production.
use_cases.target_user: Developers and designers prototyping motion graphics

prompt_templates.title: Simple Bouncing Ball
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a simple animated GIF of a bouncing ball using PIL. The ball should start at the top and bounce to the bottom with easing. Save it as bounce.gif at 128x128 with 10 FPS.

prompt_templates.title: Pulsing Star Animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an animated GIF of a yellow star that pulses (scales up and down) using the ease_in_out_easing function. Make it 128x128, 12 frames, at 10 FPS. Use gradient background.

prompt_templates.title: Particle Explosion
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an animated GIF showing particles exploding outward from center, then fading. Use numpy for particle positions, apply gravity effect, and optimize to under 128KB for Slack emoji.

prompt_templates.title: Complex Multi-Element Scene
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an animated scene with multiple moving elements: a bouncing ball, a rotating star, and a pulsing background gradient. Combine using frame_composer utilities. Validate the output meets Slack emoji requirements.

output_examples.input: Create a 128x128 emoji GIF with a bouncing ball
output_examples.output: A bouncing ball animation file saved to bounce.gif with 12 frames at 10 FPS, optimized to 48 colors for small file size

output_examples.input: Create a pulsing star with gradient background
output_examples.output: Animated star with vertical gradient background (blue to purple), smooth pulse effect using sine wave scaling

output_examples.input: Validate my GIF for Slack emoji
output_examples.output: Validation report showing dimensions, file size, frame count, and whether it passes Slack requirements

best_practices: Use dimensions of 128x128 for emoji and 480x480 for message GIFs to meet Slack requirements
Limit colors to 48 or fewer and frames to 12 for emoji-optimized GIFs under size limits
Apply easing functions (ease_out, bounce) for natural-looking motion instead of linear movement
anti_patterns: Using dimensions larger than 128x128 for emoji GIFs will exceed Slack file size limits
Creating animations without easing functions results in choppy, unnatural motion
Forgetting to validate before uploading can result in rejected or cropped GIFs in Slack

faq.question: What dimensions should I use for Slack emoji?
faq.answer: Use 128x128 pixels for emoji GIFs. Message GIFs can be up to 480x480 pixels.

faq.question: How do I reduce GIF file size?
faq.answer: Reduce the number of colors (48 or fewer), lower FPS (10 instead of 30), reduce dimensions to 128x128, and use the remove_duplicates option.

faq.question: Can I use this skill to animate uploaded images?
faq.answer: Yes, load uploaded images with PIL Image.open() and use them as base frames or inspiration for your animations.

faq.question: What easing functions are available?
faq.answer: Available functions include linear, ease_in, ease_out, ease_in_out, bounce, elastic, back, and anticipate.

faq.question: How do I validate my GIF meets Slack requirements?
faq.answer: Use the validate_gif() or is_slack_ready() functions from core.validators to check dimensions, size, and frame count.

faq.question: Does this skill upload GIFs to Slack automatically?
faq.answer: No, this skill generates the GIF file. You will need to upload it to Slack manually or use a separate Slack integration.
